role_summary: Designer of the Black Panther Party logo at Stokely Carmichael's request (1966-67); SNCC Freedom Summer recruiter; co-editor of the Student Voice newsletter; later staff at the Center for Constitutional Rights.
ai_reading: Zellner clusters in the SNCC-allied-women cohort: top neighbors are Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53, count 181), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7), Charles McLaurin (entry 17), John Churchville (entry 63), and Judy Richardson (entry 69). The embedding agrees with her cultural placement as a Freedom Summer recruiter and Student Voice co-editor. The novel observation is on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis, where Zellner sits at +0.50, well toward Strategic Vision. The cultural record would not have predicted strategic-vision testimony from someone whose single most cited contribution is the graphic design of the Black Panther Party logo (a concrete tactical artifact), but the embedding reads her broader testimony, including the recruiting and the Center for Constitutional Rights work, as strategic-positional rather than tactical-operational.
biographical_paragraph: In 1966-67, when Stokely Carmichael asked for a graphic mark for the Black Panther Party, Dottie Zellner designed the logo that became the party's iconography [src: 1]. She had come to that work through SNCC, where she served as a recruiter for the 1964 Freedom Summer project and was co-editor of the organization's newsletter, the Student Voice [src: 1]. Born in 1938 in Manhattan, New York to Jewish immigrant parents from a secular, Yiddish-speaking, social-justice-oriented left, she graduated from Queens College, City University of New York [src: 1]. She married fellow SNCC activist Bob Zellner in 1962; the marriage later ended [src: 1]. Her later career took several turns: years working as a nurse, then in 1984 a move to the Center for Constitutional Rights, and in 1998 a directorship of publications and development at Queens College School of Law [src: 1]. Since 2002 she has been active in Israeli-Palestinian peace organizing and the Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions movement [src: 1]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 30 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: The Student Voice that Zellner co-edited was SNCC's principal in-house publication, founded in June 1960 under the editorial direction of Marion Barry (SNCC's first chair) and serving as the organization's documentary record of field-staff actions, arrests, congressional testimony, and movement strategy through to its final October 1965 issue [src: 2]. Zellner's recruiting work for the 1964 Freedom Summer project (the project that brought approximately one thousand mostly-Northern white college volunteers into Mississippi for the summer of 1964 to expand the COFO voter-registration drive) ran through the Western College for Women orientation site in Oxford, Ohio (where Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney trained before their June 21, 1964 disappearance and murder by Klan members in Neshoba County, Mississippi); the volunteer-applicant screening Zellner coordinated produced the cohort the Western College sessions trained [src: 3]. Her 1966-67 logo design work for the Black Panther Party sits inside the broader visual-identity production the SNCC graphic-design tradition produced across the mid-1960s; the Lowndes County Freedom Organization black panther logo (the LCFO logo Courtland Cox catalog entry 24 documents with Jennifer Lawson at the SNCC Political Education Materials level) was the source visual the Oakland Black Panther Party founded by Newton and Seale in October 1966 derived its name and graphic identity from, and Zellner's 1966-67 work refined that lineage into the iconography the Oakland party used through the 1970s [src: 4]. Her post-SNCC career at the Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR) from 1984 placed her in the institutional successor to the LCDC (Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee) that Richard Barry Sobol (catalog entry 104) had worked with in 1960s Louisiana; CCR's 1990s and 2000s litigation against U.S. detention practices in Guantanamo Bay and in domestic immigration enforcement extended the legal-defense infrastructure tradition Zellner had been part of from the 1960s [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: Zellner's continuing public role since 2002 has centered on the Center for Constitutional Rights and the Jewish Voice for Peace organizing network; her co-edited 2014 New Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (Faith S. Holsaert and Martha Prescod Norman Noonan among the co-editors) is the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort, including her own contribution on the Student Voice editorship and the Freedom Summer recruiting work [src: 6]. Her brother-in-law Bob Zellner (the SNCC field secretary whose 2008 NewSouth Books memoir The Wrong Side of Murder Creek documents his SNCC Albany and Birmingham years, and whose marriage to Dottie ran 1962 through their later divorce) is documented at the SNCC Digital Gateway as a separate institutional record [src: 7]. The 2014 SNCC 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University in Raleigh (the conference Courtland Cox catalog entry 24 anchored) is the institutional moment her SNCC alumni network last reconvened at scale; the post-conference memoir-and-archive work has anchored her continuing public-facing organizing role [src: 8].

gallery.alt: The Student Voice newsletter, August 19, 1964, front page
gallery.caption: The Student Voice, SNCC's principal in-house publication, August 19, 1964, front page. The August 19 issue covered the murders of CORE civil rights activists James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner by Ku Klux Klan members in Neshoba County, Mississippi the previous June 21; the cover image of hands wrapped in chains reaching for voting machine levers framed the Freedom Summer voter-registration project Zellner had recruited the volunteers for. Held by the Seattle Public Library collection and digitized through the Digital Public Library of America (DPLA).
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Seattle Public Library and DPLA)
